Subjective
ere today for Ear Problem (Both ear ringing for 1 month)
History of Present Illness
The patient is a-
tinnitus, type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and allergic rhinitis.
Tinnitus
- Reports bilateral tinnitus with perceived hearing loss
- Treated for an ear infection in October 2025 at urgent care
- Referred to an otolaryngologist for persistent symptoms , was not aware it was approved
- Occasional vertigo-like symptoms triggered by head movements
- Persistent nasal congestion
- Xyzal was ineffective
Diabetes
- Last hemoglobin A1c of 8.2
- No nutritionist consultation yet, referral approved
- Eats sweets rarely, daily white rice
- Tries to stay active a few times a week

Assessment & Plan
Bilateral tinnitus:
- Reports ringing in both ears, hearing loss, occasional vertigo with head movements
- Small amount of cerumen in right ear canal
- Referral to audiology initiated
- Information on otolaryngologist provided
- Debrox for cerumen management
Type 2 diabetes mellitus:
- Uncontrolled diabetes, hemoglobin A1c 8.2
- Advised to reduce rice and sweets intake
- Interested in nutritionist consultation
- Increase metformin dosage to 3 tablets daily
Hypertension:
- Elevated blood pressure
- Advised to monitor blood pressure regularly
- Follow-up with recorded readings
Allergic rhinitis:
- Nasal congestion and vertigo likely related to allergic rhinitis
- Initiate daily Allegra and Flonase
Assessment & Plan
Behind on immunizations

claude_vision.referral_reason: Bilateral tinnitus with perceived hearing loss, occasional vertigo triggered by head movements, and eustachian tube dysfunction. Patient was initially referred to ENT/otolaryngology for persistent ear symptoms following an ear infection treated in October 2025, and subsequently referred again on 12/16/2025 after symptoms persisted including bilateral ringing, hearing loss, and vertigo.
relevant_clinical_findings: Bilateral tinnitus ongoing for approximately 1 month
Perceived hearing loss in both ears
Occasional vertigo-like symptoms triggered by head movements
History of ear infection treated at urgent care in October 2025 with Augmentin and prednisone
Persistent nasal congestion (likely contributing via eustachian tube dysfunction)
Small amount of cerumen found in right ear canal on exam (12/16/2025)
Left tympanic membrane normal on exam (12/16/2025)
Xyzal was ineffective for nasal symptoms
Eustachian tube dysfunction noted — fullness and vertigo
Allergic rhinitis with nasal congestion identified as possible contributing factor
Debrox (carbamide peroxide) prescribed for cerumen management in right ear
Referral to audiology also initiated by PCP — hearing test required before ENT visit per scheduling notes
Patient was not aware prior ENT referral had been approved
missing_information: Audiology/hearing test results — hearing test has been ordered but results are not yet available; ENT scheduling note states hearing test must be completed first
Results of the audiology referral ordered on 12/16/2025 are not attached
No CT or MRI imaging of the temporal bones or sinuses referenced or attached
No urgent care records from October 2025 ear infection visit attached
Insurance authorization details not fully visible in the referral document
Referring physician direct contact information not explicitly listed on the fax cover sheet (only general admin fax/phone provided)

provider_urgency_label.label: urgent
provider_urgency_label.source: Referral notes section (page 1 of referral document, 12/18/2025 4:12 PM entry by Lee, Caroline): 'Urgent referral and notes attached'; attachment labeled 'URG' on document dated 12/18/2025 1612 by Lee, Caroline. Also noted in triage routing: 'P Scc Sac Ent Triage Expo; P Scc Sac Ent Npc Expo'.

criteria_check.provider_label: Urgent — labeled 'Urgent referral and notes attached' and attachment marked 'URG' by Lee, Caroline on 12/18/2025
criteria_check.reasoning: The referring provider marked this referral as urgent, but the clinical content does not match any of the defined ENT urgent criteria — the tinnitus and hearing loss are described as gradual/progressive over approximately one month (not sudden acute onset), there is no confirmed or suspected malignancy, no airway compromise, no peritonsillar abscess, no foreign body, and no acute nasal fracture within the surgical window; therefore this is Tier 2 requiring secondary approval to determine whether the urgency label reflects clinical information not captured in the document.

claude_vision.next_steps: Secondary review needed — provider marked this referral urgent ('URG'), but the clinical content does not meet defined ENT urgent criteria, as the hearing loss and tinnitus appear gradual over one month rather than sudden onset; triage team should confirm with the referring provider whether urgency is clinically justified before scheduling. Note: per the 12/19/2025 scheduling comment, a hearing test must be completed first, with the ENT appointment scheduled 15 minutes after the audiology test.
claude_vision.summary: This is a referral to ENT/otolaryngology for a patient presenting with bilateral tinnitus, perceived hearing loss, and occasional vertigo for approximately one month, initially following an ear infection in October 2025. The PCP (Dr. Doncheva, Elica Health Centers) assessed eustachian tube dysfunction and allergic rhinitis as contributing factors, ordered a hearing test via audiology referral, and the scheduling team has noted that the hearing test must be completed before the ENT new patient visit. The referring provider labeled the referral as urgent ('URG'), but the clinical presentation does not clearly meet standard ENT urgent criteria, warranting secondary triage review before scheduling.
